articles.title: The Middle Power Delusion
articles.content:
今年1月，加拿大总理马克·卡尼在达沃斯世界经济论坛上警告各国领导人，称处于华盛顿和北京之间的国家需要停止单打独斗。“如果我们不在谈判桌前，我们就会成为菜单上的选项，”他说。这句话精准地捕捉了当下的情绪。在各国首都和各类会议中，中等强国突然重新流行起来。智库报告和报纸专栏将印度描述为关键的摇摆国家；将巴西、印尼、沙特阿拉伯和土耳其标榜为成功对冲的典范；并敦促澳大利亚、加拿大、欧洲、日本和韩国加强协调，减少对美国的依赖。一种新的词汇应运而生：战略自主、多边结盟、小多边主义、可变几何。通常的解释是，所有这些活动标志着多极世界的到来。美国正在失去其控制力。其他大国的崛起为西方主导的秩序提供了替代选择。旧的等级制度正在让位于一个更松散的体系，中间国家可以在其中讨价还价、居中调停，并利用大国之间的竞争。

但这种解读将焦虑误认为是实力。中等强国之所以更加引人注目，并非因为它们更强大，而是因为它们更加暴露。近年来，许多中等强国得以繁荣发展的条件正在消失。多年来，它们可以躲在美式霸权的保护下，利用全球经济的扩张，与敌对大国进行贸易而无需在它们之间做出选择。它们可以享受规模经济的好处，而无需自己拥有这种规模。但这样的世界正在消失。增长放缓，全球化已成为对关键节点的争夺，而大国变得更具掠夺性。美国越来越愿意利用其主导地位来获取让步。中国则通过补贴和出口过剩来去工业化其他国家，通过债务和基础设施使其依赖，并通过军事骚扰和经济制裁来限制其选择。结果不是中等强国崛起的扁平世界，而是一个更严酷的世界，其中两个顶级大国有更多方式迫使其他国家屈服。

危险在于中等强国会以象征性措施而非战略来应对这一新现实。峰会和伙伴关系可以营造自主的表象，但无法替代真正的实力，而真正的实力越来越依赖于融资、建设并指挥大型技术、工业、情报、物流和军事系统的能力。大多数国家也不能简单地在中美之间摇摆，从一个国家购买安全，从另一个国家购买商品，从两个国家都获得市场准入。随着竞争的加剧，对冲将开始看起来像背叛。华盛顿和北京将通过限制技术、重新安排供应链、扣留投资、阻止资金、提高关税或威胁军事报复来迫使各国表明立场。在一个日益等级化的世界中，中间地带不是开放的市场，而是一片雷区。

中等强国仍然有牌可打。许多国家控制着美国和中国所需的资产：资源、军事基地、港口、工厂、技术、军队等。但这些利基并不能保证自主权。只有在更大的保护、技术、金融和市场体系中，这些利基才能带来安全和繁荣。因此，前进的道路不是绕过华盛顿和北京的无休止的联盟购物，而是结盟：选择提供最佳庇护的强国体系，建设国家实力，并利用这种实力在联盟内部争取影响力。这排除了自由代理的幻想，但保留了更宝贵的东西：在更危险的世界中生存和繁荣的能力。

在有记录的历史中，中等强国一直是濒危物种。从大约公元前200年到1800年，任何时刻，超过一半的人类都生活在三到五个帝国的统治之下。中等政治实体存在，但经常被帝国中心反复吞并和驱逐。欧洲是最大的例外。在西罗马帝国于五世纪崩溃后，再也没有统治者能控制超过约五分之一的欧洲人口。但分裂并没有让欧洲对中等强国更安全。它产生了一个残酷的竞技场，战争造就了国家，国家也造就了战争。竞争淘汰了弱者，强化了强者，最终产生了工业化的掠食者。

到1900年，大约500个欧洲政治实体已减少到约20个，这些国家建立了覆盖地球陆地表面约85%的帝国。只有在两次世界大战摧毁了这个帝国秩序之后，中等强国才得以繁荣。战争削弱和贬低了大国，帮助将曾经的臣民转变为主权国家。工业化已经通过铁路、电报、教育、大规模生产和扩大的官僚机构将社会紧密联系在一起。世界大战通过动员数百万民众，包括殖民地居民，进入大规模军队、国家经济和中央政府，加速了这一进程。

1945年之后，许多社会将战争锻造的组织和民族主义意识转向反对帝国统治。结果是历史性的逆转：国家不再被帝国吸收，而是帝国分裂成国家。主权国家的数量激增，最终翻了四倍，创造了数十个潜在的中等强国。冷战将非殖民化变成了一段持续的中等强国时刻。在冷战中，两个超级大国都希望在去殖民化中获得影响力，因此有动力承认新国家，保护较弱的伙伴，并在其中竞争影响力。美国在北美、西欧和东亚第一岛链（从日本到台湾再到菲律宾）上建立了安全和经济保护伞。美国驻外军队、开放市场，并向盟友提供资本和技术。美国主导的秩序并非在任何地方都仁慈：美国帮助推翻了智利、危地马拉和伊朗的政府，并在越南战争期间将印度支那变成了战场。但对澳大利亚、加拿大、日本和西德等盟友来说，美国的霸权提供了庇护。它给了他们富裕、安全和影响力的空间，而无需成为大国。苏联的霸权更加严酷和贫困。它扼杀了东欧的自主权，并在非洲、亚洲和中东的部分地区引发了革命暴力。然而，它也帮助创造了一个中等强国的世界。莫斯科支持非殖民化，武装和补贴友好政权，并在东欧建立了工业能力。苏联并没有直接吞并中等国家，而是通过卫星政权间接统治，如保加利亚、捷克斯洛伐克、东德、匈牙利和波兰，以及欧洲以外的客户国，如古巴和越南。许多苏联伙伴几乎没有真正的独立，但它们保留了边界、官僚机构、军队、工业基地和国际机构的席位。

这些相互竞争的霸权为中等强国的时刻提供了安全基础。1945年之前，国家经常被从地图上抹去。1945年之后，国家的消亡变得罕见，从大约每三年一次减少到大约每30年一次。对许多国家来说，征服的风险降至历史最低水平。生存只是中等强国时刻的第一个条件。真正让受保护的国家变得繁荣和有影响力的是历史上最大的全球增长浪潮，工业化远远超出了其最初的西方核心。几千年来，大多数社会都生活在接近生存的边缘，被稀缺的能源供应、低农业生产力、健康状况差和寿命短所阻碍。工业化通过利用化石燃料、机械和现代基础设施打破了这一上限。冷战后，后发国家不再需要从头开始建设现代经济。它们可以借用其他地方发明的技术，进口机器，复制已证明的生产方法，将工人从农场转移到工厂，从电气化、卫生设施、城市化和大规模制造中获益。对于新兴大国来说，这创造了一个工业化的阶梯。美国主导的秩序使这个阶梯更容易攀登。在美式保护下，数十个国家可以繁荣发展，而无需夺取殖民地、建设远洋海军或完全保护自己的供应链。美国保持了海上通道的畅通，锚定了以美元为基础的金融体系，并为一个资本、商品、能源和技术流动异常顺畅的世界提供了支持，特别是随着集装箱运输和数字协调的引入，全球生产得以扩张。那些可能曾经被小市场、危险的邻国或有限资源所困的国家可以接入一个他们自己无需维护的全球经济。

墨西哥、波兰、韩国、土耳其和越南成为制造节点。澳大利亚、巴西、智利、印尼、海湾国家和南非乘着商品繁荣的东风。印度和菲律宾通过提供服务获得分量，而爱尔兰、新加坡和阿联酋成为商业中心。路线各不相同，但结果相似：低于大国层级的国家可以享受全球规模的收益，而无需拥有全球力量。全球化使增长具有传染性。一个国家的起飞成为另一个国家的出口市场、投资机会或商品繁荣。中国的崛起加速了这一进程。其经济拥有超过五分之一的人口，以近两位数的年增长率增长，购买了中等强国的大部分商品，并释放了世界前所未见的需求冲击。1990年至2008年，全球产出在名义美元基础上几乎翻了三倍，全球贸易增长了四倍多。这次繁荣使中等强国受益最多。在本世纪初的十年中，新兴经济体平均每年增长近6%，几乎是美国的三倍。大约三分之二的国家增长速度超过美国的两倍。换句话说，世界上大部分地区不仅变得更富有，而且在追赶。全球化似乎解决了旧的中等强国问题。国家不再需要帝国来获得影响力。它们可以通过融入增长的全球经济变得更有钱、更连通、更重要。由此产生的“其余国家的崛起”似乎预示着多极时代的到来。中等强国不仅在增长，而且在组织上。欧盟向东扩张，被广泛讨论为潜在的超级大国。金砖国家从华尔街的缩写（巴西、俄罗斯、印度和中国）演变为外交俱乐部，为权力从西方转移提供了制度形式。商品繁荣提升了OPEC的影响力。要求扩大联合国安理会的呼声增强。2008年之后，G20取代了G7成为全球危机管理的主要论坛。一个不再由少数大国主导的世界突然变得可能。但现在，中等强国时刻的基础正在崩塌。霸权庇护正在削弱，超全球化正在解体，快速增长正在放缓。这种模式适用于中等强国，无论是从物质上定义为美国和中国之后的20个最大经济体，还是从政治上定义为在华盛顿和北京之间周旋的国家。无论是哪种方式，旧的支持都在瓦解。首先消失的是容易的增长。中等强国现在的增长速度比1990年至2008年期间的繁荣期慢了大约四分之一到三分之一，使典型经济体比旧节奏持续下去要小20%以上。它们也停止了与美国的追赶。许多国家在2000年代初将其经济权重翻倍，此后大多数国家已经回落了三分之一。债务负担比2005年高出约四分之一，自2008年以来，这些国家中约有三分之二的生产率增长转为负值。这不仅仅是经济周期的问题。提升中等强国的电梯正在停滞，因为许多最容易获得的收益已经被实现。国家只能铺高速公路、通电村庄、建港口、将工人从农场转移到工厂一次。此后，增长更多地依赖于创新，而创新更难产生，传播也更慢。新技术，包括人工智能，尚未带来与早期工业突破相当的生产率提升。人口结构加剧了这一问题。

目前，中等强国中约有四分之三面临生育率低于更替水平、劳动适龄人口减少或停滞以及老年人口在未来25年内预计平均翻倍的问题。这些不利因素使“其他崛起国家”趋势出现逆转。自2008年以来的全球经济增长放缓，也促使武装最重的国家加强对市场、资源、技术和领土的控制。俄罗斯试图将其邻国纳入一个受控制的经济圈；大约在2010年，它开始推动后苏联国家加入由莫斯科主导的关税同盟，这将降低俄罗斯商品的贸易壁垒，同时提高对西方的贸易壁垒。当乌克兰试图通过与欧盟达成自由贸易协议来抵制时，莫斯科施加了经济压力，随后于2014年入侵。中国通过债务刺激、工业补贴、出口过剩、海外放贷（最终演变为严厉的债务回收）以及台湾和南海地区的军事建设来应对增长放缓。与此同时，美国变得更加务实，通过关税、制裁、产业政策和军事力量与盟友和对手进行更艰难的谈判。超全球化曾让中等国家在没有认真保卫边境、供应链或市场份额的情况下繁荣发展。如今，中等国家不再能像以前那样轻易获得大国的恩惠。在冷战期间，意识形态的忠诚是有价值的。较弱的国家作为象征性的多米诺骨牌、军事基地或缓冲区，对美苏集团之间的断层线具有重要意义，使它们能够为援助、武器、市场准入和外交支持进行谈判。埃及、印度、巴基斯坦、南斯拉夫等国都参与了这场游戏。超级大国还通过廉价能源、优惠贸易、信贷、武器和援助来维持其集团——每年转让价值数十亿美元。如今的中美竞争有所不同。华盛顿和北京并非在铁幕之后建立对立的世界；他们的目标不是以任何代价购买忠诚，而是控制他人依赖的系统：金融、技术、矿物、能源、航运和数据。从表面上看，这种战略似乎有利于控制关键节点的中等国家。台湾主导尖端芯片制造，荷兰制造先进光刻机，韩国在存储芯片方面领先，智利在铜和锂方面占据巨大份额，新加坡是全球航运中心，土耳其控制着黑海与地中海之间的海峡——这个名单还在继续。这些资产使中等国家拥有影响力。但影响力并不等于独立。一个控制关键节点的国家可以扰乱一个系统。一个控制多个系统中多个节点的国家可以决定谁获得准入，以什么条件和价格。欧盟是美国霸权的产物，而不是其替代品。这是利基影响力和结构性力量之间的区别。美国具有结构性力量。美元主导全球金融。美国消费者市场比接下来的七个国家的总和还要大。美国公司约占全球风险投资的一半，全球高科技收入的一半以上。美国是全球石油和天然气的主要生产国，是唯一能够远离本土作战的主要国家，为大约70个国家提供安全保障。中等国家可能拥有关键工厂、资源、港口或技术，但如果它需要美元、客户、能源、保护、软件或云服务，它仍然必须与华盛顿打交道。美国对中国的半导体控制说明了这种等级制度。盟友生产芯片供应链中不可或缺的部分，但美国在设计、软件、设备、云平台、金融、终端市场和出口控制规则中占据主导地位。2022年华盛顿实施重大芯片限制后，盟友抗议并寻求公司救济。但日本和荷兰最终采取了并行限制，韩国和台湾的企业仍需美国授权才能继续运营其在中国的工厂。特朗普于2025年4月宣布的“解放日”关税显示了同样的模式。中等国家对几乎每个美国贸易伙伴征收关税感到愤怒。但很少有国家联合起来应对，更少的国家迫使华盛顿退让。大多数国家在政策让步、购买美国商品和政策让步方面进行了双边谈判，以换取投资承诺。他们可以就美国压力的条款进行谈判，但不能逃避压力本身。BRICS是一个不满的联盟。中国创造了同样的等级制度。中国缺乏华盛顿的金融和安全覆盖，但其工业规模使其他国家进入以中国为中心的供应链。中国的国家银行可以为大规模的基础设施和工业项目提供资金，其工厂大约生产了世界三分之一的制成品，在造船、电池、电动汽车、无人机、太阳能电池板和稀土加工方面占据主导地位。这使北京有多种方式挤压中等国家。它可以购买原材料，向市场投放廉价出口，扣留资金或建筑支持未完成的项目，并利用外国工厂对中国的依赖。印尼有镍，但中国公司控制着大部分精炼厂。墨西哥和越南受益于供应链从中国转移，但许多工厂仍依赖中国零部件。中等国家可能控制着有价值的系统部分，但中国往往控制着周围的工业生态系统。中等国家的军事力量同样具有等级制度。无人机、导弹、地雷和网络攻击使中等国家的爪牙更加锋利。但近身战斗与海外力量投送不同。过去一年的美国行动显示了这种差异。在委内瑞拉，美国花费数月时间监视该国领导人尼古拉斯·马杜罗的动向，然后从20个地点发射了150多架飞机，击落了加拉加斯的部分电力，使委内瑞拉的防御系统失效，并迅速将特种部队和FBI特工通过直升机运送到首都，以夺取马杜罗并将其带回美国军舰。美国和以色列情报部门追踪伊朗领导人的动向，并在他们分散之前击中他们。网络和空间部队使伊朗指挥中心失明。随后，超过100架美国飞机从陆地和海上发射，击中了1000多个目标，摧毁了大部分伊朗高级领导层，并摧毁了该国的防空、空军、海军和导弹部队。当德黑兰开火时，美国船员拦截了数百枚导弹和无人机，瞄准了船只和海湾基地。这体现了干扰与指挥的区别。一些中等国家可以制造更强大的军队。但没有一个国家能够持续追踪数千个目标，长距离移动部队，保护他们，为他们提供燃料和重新武装，融合跨领域的智能，并在远离本土的地方继续战斗数周或数月。即使成功的抵抗通常取决于一个更大的系统。例如，乌克兰表现出色，但只有通过西方的货币、情报、防空、训练、通信和弹药网络才能实现。如果中等国家重新回到谈判桌上，显而易见的回应是联合起来。这是卡尼在达沃斯的讲话内容。这种冲动是可以理解的。联盟可以放大中等国家的声音，使它们在特定问题上拥有影响力。但它们不能将中等国家变成大国，也不能保证它们在谈判桌上拥有永久席位。首要问题是规模。世界并非多极化。在权力的核心衡量标准上，美国远远超过其他所有国家。中国通常排名第二，而其他国家则远远落后。前两名之间的差距远大于中等国家之间的差距。这种陡峭的下滑意味着即使是最广泛的中等国家联盟也无法形成一个极点。哈佛大学贝弗中心将13个“中等国家”指定为：巴西、埃及、印度、印度尼西亚、哈萨克斯坦、尼日利亚、巴基斯坦、沙特阿拉伯、新加坡、南非、土耳其、阿联酋和越南。这些国家的经济总量仍不到美国GDP的一半，约为美国消费者市场的五分之二，约占美国军事开支的四分之一，而全球高科技收入几乎为零。贝弗中心本身称其为“不是连贯的集团”。即使是幻想联盟也会落后。以任何主要的权力衡量标准来看，排名第三到第十的国家，将它们结合起来。它们仍然无法与美国匹敌。GDP仍比美国小，消费者市场小四分之一，军事开支只有三分之二，高科技收入不到一半。但规模只是第一个问题。更深层次的障碍是政治。中等国家联盟面临一个基本的权衡：它们变得越大，承载的重量就越大，但越难将它们团结在一起。小俱乐部可以迅速行动，但没有足够的影响力。要获得影响力，必须通过增加否决点、竞争对手和搭便车者来增加重量。要克服这些问题，成功的联盟通常需要一个锚点：一个愿意并能够通过吸收成本、安抚摇摆者和惩罚叛逃者来领导联盟的国家。英国在拿破仑时代扮演了这一角色。英国和后来的苏联在希特勒时代也扮演了这一角色，直到美国进入第二次世界大战。如今，没有中等国家能发挥这种作用。结果是，没有严肃的中等国家联盟存在。最容易建立中等国家联盟的方式是通过超级大国的保护。但这个解决方案创造了一个悖论。霸权庇护帮助中等国家聚集资源并克服内部矛盾，但它也削弱了它们独立行动的能力。就像温室一样，它让脆弱的花园得以生长，同时让它无法适应更恶劣的天气。欧盟体现了这种悖论。富裕且制度化程度很高的国家，看起来是世界上最有前途的中等国家联盟。但美国的保护使欧洲一体化成为可能，同时抑制了欧洲的旧安全困境。然而，它也扼杀了欧洲的强硬实力。相反，在冷战后，欧洲成为了一个福利超级大国，花费的GDP不到5%，但大约25%用于社会保护——尽管只占世界人口的5%。结果是，欧洲在没有美国保护的情况下，反复努力管理自己的大陆危机，从巴尔干到乌克兰，更不用说海外力量投送。欧洲也习惯了从美国保护的全球经济发展中购买所需，而不是在本土建立工业实力。它专注于制定规则，假设其他国家会采用其标准，但最终却使自己陷入了能源脆弱性和技术停滞。它关闭了核电厂，禁止了水力压裂，如今进口了60%的能源。在乌克兰战争之前，俄罗斯供应了欧洲一半的天然气和煤炭进口，超过四分之一的石油。从那时起，欧洲就依赖俄罗斯，现在则更加依赖美国。在技术方面，全球50家最大的科技公司中，只有四家是欧洲公司，而美国大约有30家。2013年，欧洲在全球科技收入中的份额从22%下降到18%，而美国的份额从30%上升到38%。欧洲监管了数字资本主义，但很少生产它。

没有一家欧洲公司能像亚马逊、谷歌、Meta或微软那样达到如此规模，因此欧洲的数字经济很大程度上依赖美国平台运行：云计算、商业软件、网络安全、人工智能系统、智能手机操作系统和支付系统。总体趋势是相对衰落。2008年，欧盟经济规模大于美国，占全球GDP的25%。到2024年，美国经济规模比欧盟大三分之一，其全球GDP占比降至17%。共识规则往往使东盟沦为外交等候室。其他中等强国联盟则更弱。金砖国家（BRICS）最初由巴西、俄罗斯、印度、中国和南非组成，后扩展包括更多新成员和伙伴国，如伊朗。但金砖国家并未成为大国胁迫的制衡力量，反而成为抱怨集团，将中等强国与中国和俄罗斯——许多成员希望对冲的霸权国家——联合起来。它们既怨恨西方主导地位，也互不信任：印度担心中国，伊朗与海湾国家冲突，大多数国家更喜欢灵活性而非集团纪律。今年美国和以色列袭击伊朗时，金砖国家甚至无法就联合声明达成一致。东南亚国家联盟（ASEAN）现为11国组织，也并不更强。其成员没有共同威胁、战略或经济基础。越南和菲律宾害怕中国，而柬埔寨和老挝依赖中国，印尼捍卫自主权，新加坡对冲，缅甸内战。共识规则让任何成员都能阻止行动，常使东盟沦为外交等候室。较小的集团看起来更有希望，因为它们做得更少。欧佩克（OPEC）曾显示中等强国控制集中、不可或缺资源时可以发挥真正杠杆作用。但欧佩克是单一商品卡特尔，不是地缘政治集团。其成员希望高油价，而非共同政治秩序——即使这种狭隘交易也在松动，安哥拉、卡塔尔和阿联酋已退出该组织。全面与进步跨太平洋伙伴关系协定（CPTPP）是贸易协议，不是战略联盟。国际组织内的小团体，如世界贸易组织的渥太华集团，是技术论坛，但不是权力替代品。相比之下，最有效的多边主义只是证明规则。四方安全对话（Quad）将澳大利亚、印度、日本和美国联系在安全合作上。硅盾（Pax Silica）是技术供应链倡议。两者都有效，因为华盛顿是锚点。这使得卡尼在达沃斯演讲中提出的选项：“可变几何”，即临时拼凑联盟。但这不是中等强国秩序。这是常规世界政治：国家在压力下争夺，而强国则分裂、贿赂、威胁和惩罚任何试图绕过它们的联盟。一些学者想象一种自助式秩序，中等强国在此自由选择安全、技术或市场准入。但世界不是商场。它是自然状态。2019年欧洲建立机制INSTEX绕过美国制裁并继续与伊朗贸易后，华盛顿威胁其用户被驱逐出美元系统。同年土耳其购买俄罗斯防空系统后，华盛顿将其驱逐出F-35项目。2025年，印度继续购买俄罗斯石油，尽管美国警告不要，华盛顿对其征收50%关税。中国同样积极执行其等级制度。它迫使柬埔寨阻止东盟批评北京在南中国海的军事扩张，惩罚立陶宛与台湾接触，通过限制贸易和针对立陶宛在全球供应链中的投入。澳大利亚因寻求新冠疫情起源调查而受到中国关税和非正式禁令（大麦、葡萄酒、牛肉、煤炭、棉花和龙虾）影响。它还迫使越南通过威胁对抗和海上民兵船只包围钻井平台，停止与西班牙、阿联酋、俄罗斯和日本相关公司进行海上天然气项目。在大国冲突加剧的世界中，可变几何无法保护中等强国。它暴露了它们，因为任何足够强大的联盟都可见到惩罚。如果中等强国无法独立、形成一极或隐藏在临时联盟中，它们必须选择一个更大的系统来依靠。这不需要盲目服从。它们仍可广泛贸易并与其他国家对话。但在核心权力问题上——购买谁的武器、分享谁的情报、依靠谁的银行、建立谁的芯片和云平台、加入谁的能源网络、执行谁的制裁——它们将越来越需要选边。对冲在威胁遥远、大国容忍模糊时有效。但当竞争加剧，双方开始问相同问题：你是否支持我们？对中等强国来说，挑战不再是避免选择。而是如何选择赞助人而不成为棋子。对齐不是屈服。它是将利基转化为杠杆的策略。单独来看，中等强国的资产——港口、基地、芯片制造工具、矿藏、无人机产业、造船厂——可能不足以保证国家的安全。但在更大联盟内，这些利基可以成为中等强国缺乏的谈判筹码：保护、情报、技术、资本、市场准入和战略影响力。重点不是逃避依赖，这通常不可能，而是使依赖互惠。一个国家如果对超级大国证明自己有用，它可以成为超级大国咨询、武装、资助和捍卫的伙伴。建立中等强国联盟的最简单方法是在超级大国保护下。日本展示了这一点。正如迈克尔·格林最近在《外交事务》中解释的，东京不是试图取代美国在亚洲的权力；而是使自己不可或缺。日本给华盛顿提供其在该地区竞争所需：本地基地、技术、工业能力、船舶维修、导弹生产、组织联盟帮助和区域合法性，使美国战略看起来不像是外部干预，而是由主要亚洲民主国家领导的联盟。作为回报，日本获得进入唯一能平衡中国的军事力量，以及在使用该力量方面有更大发言权。芬兰和瑞典在欧洲做出类似选择。它们加入北约不是因为忘记如何自卫，而是因为国家韧性在美式权力支持下更有效。北约获得高度能干的北方军队，芬兰和瑞典获得第五条保护。澳大利亚、波兰、菲律宾和韩国是同一主题的变体：每个国家都在建立国家实力，同时使自己在以美国为首的网络中更有价值。这就是为什么联盟不同于大多数国际安全组织，实际有效。集体安全机构，如已解散的国际联盟和联合国，旨在要求国家在抽象上捍卫规则——对抗任何侵略者、任何地点、是否看到直接利益。可变几何更加脆弱，要求国家在危险出现时基本上临时应对。联盟相比之下更实用。它们指明威胁、汇集能力、分配角色、在危机前建立合作习惯。它们的粘合剂不是普遍善意。而是共同危险。这可能是一个丑陋的动机，但国家在知道合作对象时更可靠地合作。选择正确的对齐不是自动的。中等强国不应盲目依附最强或最熟悉的国家。它们应问一个更难的问题：哪个大国对它们的安全、繁荣和自主权构成更大威胁？答案会有所不同。一些国家面临入侵。其他国家面临经济胁迫、技术依赖、政治干预或不可靠赞助人抛弃。少数大型中等强国，如印度，可能比大多数保留更多操作空间。但随着大国竞争加剧，即使是中等强国也必须决定最需要逃避的危险和提供最多避难所的更大系统。任何足够强大的联盟都可见到惩罚。对大多数中等强国来说，这是在较小邪恶中的选择，但应是容易的。美国是越来越难的赞助人。它用关税、制裁、出口管制、对领土军事准入的突然政策转变来恐吓盟友。但美国仍提供其他力量无法匹敌的东西：唯一能进行主要远方战争的军事力量；进入世界最深资本市场、最大消费者基础、领先创新中心；进入一个由富裕、有能力盟友组成的大型俱乐部。同样重要的是，美国权力通过民主机构运行，外部人士有时可以影响。盟友可以游说国会、动员公司、塑造媒体辩论、与其他美国伙伴进行交易。他们可能不会赢，但可以参与。中国提供更薄的交易。北京可以修建道路、资助港口、购买商品和工业零件。对一些国家来说，这有用。但除了贷款、基础设施和对西方的杠杆作用外，北京提供很少：没有安全保护伞、储备货币或开放政治渠道，通过这些较弱伙伴可以讨价还价。其权力通过围栏运作。它资助港口、供应建筑公司、建设网络、加工矿物、用出口洪水市场，寻求购买更少。开始时的发展可能变成附庸。最终，中等强国无法选择是否生活在等级世界中。它们必须选择哪个等级给它们更多操作空间。危险是将自主权表现误认为权力实质——庆祝峰会、论坛和鼓舞人心的演讲，而真正的金钱、技术、能源和力量杠杆在强者手中积累。安全不会来自独立或拼凑临时联盟，而是有效谈判在更大系统内。中等强国不是平坦世界中的自由代理人。但它们仍可以通过与日益不平等的超级大国合作而繁荣。Adblock测试（为什么？）
articles.summary:
In January, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney warned leaders gathered at the World Economic Forum in Davos that states caught between Washington and Beijing needed to stop negotiating alone. “If we’re not at the table,” he said, “we’re on the menu.” The line captured the mood of the moment. Across capitals and conferences, middle powers are suddenly back in fashion. Think tank reports and newspaper columns describe India as a pivotal swing state; hold up Brazil, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey as models of successful hedging; and urge Australia, Canada, Europe, Japan, and South Korea to coordinate more and rely less on the United States. A new vocabulary has followed: strategic autonomy, multialignment, minilateralism, variable geometry.

The usual interpretation is that all this activity marks the arrival of a multipolar world. The United States is losing its grip. The rise of the rest has created alternatives to the Western-dominated order. The old hierarchy is giving way to a looser system in which states in the middle can bargain, broker, and play the great powers off one another.

But this reading mistakes anxiety for strength. Middle powers are not becoming more visible because they are more powerful. They are becoming more visible because they are more exposed. The conditions that allowed many of them to flourish in recent decades are eroding. For years, they could shelter under U.S. hegemony, exploit an expanding global economy, and trade with rival powers without choosing among them. They could reap the benefits of scale without possessing it themselves.

That world is disappearing. Growth has slowed, globalization has become a contest over chokepoints, and great powers have grown more predatory. The United States is increasingly willing to use its dominance to extract concessions. China is using subsidies and export gluts to deindustrialize other countries, debt and infrastructure to make them dependent, and military harassment and economic sanctions to narrow their choices. The result is not a flatter world of ascendant middle powers but a harsher one in which the two top powers have more ways to bend others to their will.

The danger is that middle powers will respond to this new reality with symbolism instead of strategy. Summits and partnerships can create the appearance of autonomy, but they cannot substitute for raw power, which increasingly depends on the ability to finance, build, and command large systems of technology, industry, intelligence, logistics, and force. Nor can most states simply float between the United States and China, buying security from one, goods from the other, and market access from both. As rivalry hardens, hedging will start to look like betrayal. Washington and Beijing will make states show where they stand by restricting technology, rerouting supply chains, withholding intelligence, blocking investment, raising tariffs, or threatening military reprisals. In an increasingly hierarchical world, the middle is not an open marketplace. It is a minefield.

Middle powers still have cards to play. Many control assets the United States and China need: resources, military bases, ports, factories, technologies, armies. But these niches do not guarantee autonomy. They generate security and prosperity only when plugged into larger systems of protection, technology, finance, and markets. The path forward, then, is not endless coalition shopping to route around Washington and Beijing. It is alignment: choosing the great-power system that offers the best shelter from a country’s gravest threat, building national strength, and using that strength to bargain for influence inside the coalition. This rules out the fantasy of free agency. But it preserves something more valuable: the ability to survive and thrive in a more dangerous world.

TURNING THE TABLES

For most of recorded history, middle powers were endangered species. From roughly 200 BCE to 1800 CE, at any given moment, more than half of humanity lived under the domain of just three to five empires. Midsize polities existed but were repeatedly chewed up and spat out as imperial centers waxed and waned.

Europe was the great exception. After the Western Roman Empire collapsed in the fifth century, no ruler ever again controlled more than about a fifth of the continent’s population. But fragmentation did not make Europe safe for middle powers. It produced a brutal arena in which war made states and states made war. Competition culled the weak, hardened the strong, and eventually produced industrialized predators. By 1900, the roughly 500 European polities that existed around 1500 had been winnowed to about 20, and those powers established empires that covered roughly 85 percent of the earth’s land surface.

Middle powers are becoming more visible because they are more exposed.

Only after the two world wars shattered this imperial order did middle powers flourish. The wars weakened and discredited the great powers while helping transform once-subject peoples into sovereign nations. Industrialization had already begun knitting societies together through railroads, telegraphs, education, mass production, and expanding bureaucracies. The world wars accelerated that process by mobilizing millions, including colonial subjects, into mass armies, national economies, and centralized administrations. After 1945, many societies turned the organization and nationalist consciousness forged by war against imperial rule. The result was a historic reversal: instead of states being absorbed into empires, empires splintered into states. The number of sovereign countries surged and eventually quadrupled, creating dozens of potential middle powers.

The Cold War turned decolonization into a sustained middle-power moment. Locked in a global ideological rivalry, both superpowers had incentives to recognize new states, protect weaker partners, and compete for influence among them. The United States extended a security and economic umbrella over North America, Western Europe, and the first island chain in East Asia, stretching from Japan through Taiwan to the Philippines. Washington stationed forces abroad, opened its market, and supplied allies with capital and technology. The U.S.-led order was hardly benign everywhere: Washington helped topple governments in Chile, Guatemala, and Iran, and turned Indochina into a battlefield during the Vietnam War. But for allies such as Australia, Canada, Japan, and West Germany, U.S. hegemony provided shelter. It gave them room to grow rich, secure, and influential without becoming great powers themselves.

Soviet hegemony was harsher and poorer. It smothered autonomy in Eastern Europe and fueled revolutionary violence across parts of Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. Yet it, too, helped create a world of middle powers. Moscow backed decolonization, armed and subsidized friendly regimes, and built industrial capacity in Eastern Europe. Rather than absorbing midsize states outright, the Soviet Union often ruled them indirectly, through satellite regimes in Bulgaria, Czechoslovakia, East Germany, Hungary, and Poland, and subsidized clients outside Europe, such as Cuba and Vietnam. Many Soviet partners had little real independence, but they retained borders, bureaucracies, armies, industrial bases, and seats in international institutions.

Together, these rival hegemonies provided the security foundation for a middle-power moment. Before 1945, states were regularly wiped off the map. After 1945, state death became rare, falling from roughly one country every three years to about one every 30. For many states, the risk of conquest dropped to historically unprecedented lows.

WHEN IT RAINS, IT POURS

Survival was only the first condition of the middle-power moment. What turned protected states into prosperous and influential ones was the greatest global growth surge in history, as industrialization spread far beyond its original Western core. For millennia, most societies had lived close to subsistence, held back by scarce energy supply, low agricultural productivity, poor health, and short lifespans. Industrialization broke that ceiling by harnessing fossil fuels, machinery, and modern infrastructure. By the Cold War, late-developing countries no longer had to build the modern economy from scratch. They could borrow technologies invented elsewhere, import machinery, copy proven production methods, shift workers from farms to factories, and reap the gains from electrification, sanitation, urbanization, and mass manufacturing. For emerging powers, this created an industrial escalator.

The U.S.-led order made that escalator easier to ride. With American protection, dozens of countries could prosper without seizing colonies, building blue-water navies, or fully defending their own supply chains. The United States kept sea lanes open, anchored the dollar-based financial system, and underwrote a world in which capital, goods, energy, and technology moved with unusual ease, especially as the introduction of shipping containers and digital coordination allowed global production to expand.

Countries that might once have been trapped by small markets, dangerous neighborhoods, or limited resources could plug into a global economy they did not have to police themselves. Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Turkey, and Vietnam became manufacturing nodes. Australia, Brazil, Chile, Indonesia, the Gulf states, and South Africa rode the commodity boom. India and the Philippines gained weight by providing services, while Ireland, Singapore, and the United Arab Emirates became commercial hubs. The routes varied, but the result was similar: states below the great-power tier could reap the gains of global scale without possessing global power.

The foundations of the middle-power moment are crumbling.

Globalization then made growth contagious. One country’s takeoff became another’s export market, investment opportunity, or commodity boom. China’s rise supercharged the process. Its economy, home to more than a fifth of humanity, grew at nearly double-digit annual rates, buying much of what middle powers had to sell and unleashing a demand shock unlike anything the world had seen. Between 1990 and 2008, global economic output nearly tripled in current-dollar terms, and global trade expanded more than fourfold.

That boom lifted middle powers most. In the first decade of this century, developing economies grew by nearly six percent a year on average, almost three times the U.S. pace. Roughly two-thirds of countries grew by more than four percent, at least twice as fast as the United States. Much of the world, in other words, was not just getting richer—it was catching up. Globalization appeared to have solved the old middle-power problem. States no longer needed empire to gain influence. They could become wealthier, more connected, and more consequential simply by integrating into a growing world economy.

The resulting “rise of the rest” seemed to herald a multipolar age. Middle powers were not just growing; they were organizing. The European Union expanded eastward and was widely discussed as a potential superpower. The BRICs evolved from a Wall Street acronym for the fast-growing economies of Brazil, Russia, India, and China into a diplomatic club, giving institutional form to the idea that power was shifting away from the West. The commodity boom boosted OPEC’s leverage. Demands to expand the UN Security Council gained force. And after 2008, the G-20 displaced the G-7 as the main forum for global crisis management. A world no longer dominated by a handful of great powers suddenly seemed possible.

FROM RISE TO RUPTURE

But now the foundations of the middle-power moment are crumbling. Hegemonic shelter is weakening, hyperglobalization is unraveling, and rapid growth is slowing. This pattern holds whether middle powers are defined materially, as the 20 largest economies after the United States and China, or politically, as states trying to maneuver between Washington and Beijing. Either way, the old supports are giving way.

The first to go was easy growth. Middle powers are now growing roughly a quarter to a third more slowly than during the 1990 to 2008 boom, leaving the typical economy more than 20 percent smaller than it would have been had the old pace continued. They have also stopped catching up with the United States. Many doubled their economic weight relative to the United States in the early 2000s; since then, most have fallen back by a third. Debt burdens are about one-quarter higher than they were in 2005, and productivity growth has turned negative in roughly two-thirds of these countries since 2008.

This is not just a bad cycle. The escalator that lifted middle powers is stalling because many of the easiest gains have already been made. Countries can pave highways, electrify villages, build ports, and move workers from farms to factories only once. After that, growth depends more on innovation, which is harder to generate and slower to spread. New technologies, including artificial intelligence, have not yet delivered productivity gains on the scale of earlier industrial breakthroughs.

Demography compounds the problem. Roughly three-quarters of middle powers now have below-replacement fertility, shrinking or stagnant prime-age workforces, and senior populations set to double, on average, within 25 years. Together, these headwinds have thrown the rise of the rest into reverse.

BACK ON THE MENU

The global economic slowdown since 2008 has also motivated the most heavily armed states to assert greater control over markets, resources, technology, and territory. Russia sought to bind its neighbors into a captive economic sphere. Around 2010, it began pressing post-Soviet states to join a Moscow-led customs union that would lower barriers to Russian goods while raising barriers to the West. When Ukraine resisted by moving toward a free-trade agreement with the European Union, Moscow applied economic pressure and then invaded in 2014. China has responded to slowing growth with debt-fueled stimulus, industrial subsidies, export gluts, overseas lending that turned into harsh debt collection, and a military buildup around Taiwan and the South China Sea. The United States, meanwhile, has become more transactional, using tariffs, sanctions, industrial policy, and military power to bargain harder with both allies and adversaries. Hyperglobalization once let middle powers prosper without seriously defending their borders, supply chains, or market shares. No longer.

Middle powers also can no longer extract great-power favors as easily as they once did. During the Cold War, ideological allegiance had value. Weaker states mattered as symbolic dominoes, military bases, or buffers along the fault lines between the U.S. and Soviet blocs, allowing them to bargain for aid, arms, market access, and diplomatic support. Egypt, India, Pakistan, Yugoslavia, and others played that game. The superpowers also subsidized core allied middle powers. The United States supplied Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, and West Germany with capital, technology, and market access while tolerating the protectionist policies those countries enacted to shelter their infant industries. The Soviet Union sustained its bloc with cheap energy, preferential trade, credits, arms, and aid—transfers worth tens of billions of dollars a year.

Today’s U.S.-Chinese rivalry works differently. Washington and Beijing are not building rival worlds separated by an iron curtain; they are fighting for dominance within one global economy. Their goal is not to buy allegiance at any price but to control systems that others depend on: finance, technology, minerals, energy, shipping, and data. At first glance, that strategy might seem to favor middle powers that control chokepoints. Taiwan dominates leading-edge chipmaking, the Netherlands makes advanced lithography machines, South Korea leads in memory chips, Chile is a giant in copper and lithium, Singapore is a global shipping hub, Turkey controls the straits between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean—the list goes on. These assets give middle powers leverage. But leverage is not independence. A country that controls one critical node can disrupt a system. A country that controls many nodes across many systems can decide who gets access, on what terms, and at what price.

The EU is a product of U.S. hegemony, not an alternative to it.

That is the difference between niche leverage and structural power. The United States has structural power. The dollar dominates global finance. The U.S. consumer market is larger than those of the next seven countries combined. U.S. firms supply roughly half of global venture capital and generate more than half of global high-tech revenue. The United States is the world’s top oil and gas producer, the only country that can fight major wars far from home, and the security provider for roughly 70 countries. A middle power may have a key factory, resource, port, or technology, but if it needs U.S. dollars, customers, energy, protection, software, or cloud services, it still has to deal with Washington.

U.S. semiconductor controls on China illustrate this hierarchy. Allies produce indispensable parts of the chip supply chain, but the United States sits across the stack, in design, software, equipment, cloud platforms, finance, end markets, and export-control rules that reach foreign firms using U.S. technology. After Washington imposed major chip restrictions in 2022, allies protested and sought relief for their companies. But Japan and the Netherlands ultimately adopted parallel restrictions, and South Korean and Taiwanese firms still needed U.S. authorization to keep their China-based fabrication plants, known as fabs, operating.

Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs, announced in April 2025, showed the same pattern. Middle powers were outraged by duties imposed on nearly every U.S. trading partner, including close allies. But few mounted a collective response, and fewer still forced Washington to back down. Most negotiated bilaterally for softer versions of the tariffs, seeking lower rates, sectoral exemptions, or partial relief in exchange for investment pledges, purchases of American goods, and policy concessions. They could bargain over the terms of U.S. pressure, but they could not escape the pressure itself.

BRICS is a grievance caucus.

China creates a different version of the same hierarchy. It lacks Washington’s financial and security reach, but its industrial scale lets it pull other countries into Chinese-centered supply chains. Its state banks can finance massive infrastructure and industrial projects, and its factories produce roughly a third of the world’s manufactured goods, with dominant shares in shipbuilding, batteries, electric vehicles, drones, solar panels, and rare-earth processing. That gives Beijing many ways to squeeze middle powers. It can buy up raw materials, flood markets with cheap exports, withhold financing or construction support from unfinished projects, and exploit foreign factories’ reliance on Chinese parts. Indonesia has nickel, but Chinese firms control much of its refining. Mexico and Vietnam benefit as supply chains move out of China, but many of their factories still depend on Chinese inputs. Middle powers may control valuable pieces of the system, but China often controls the industrial ecosystem around them.

Military power is just as hierarchical. Drones, missiles, mines, and cyberattacks have given middle powers sharper claws. But mauling invaders near home is not the same as projecting power overseas. The past year of U.S. operations has shown the difference. In Venezuela, the United States spent months spying on the movements of the country’s leader, Nicolás Maduro, then launched more than 150 aircraft from 20 locations, knocked out power in parts of Caracas, disabled Venezuelan defenses, and rushed Special Forces and FBI agents into the capital by helicopter to seize Maduro and carry him back to a U.S. warship. Against Iran, U.S. and Israeli intelligence tracked the movements of Iranian leaders and hit them before they could scatter. Cyber and space forces blinded Iranian command centers. More than 100 U.S. aircraft then launched from land and sea in a synchronized wave, striking more than 1,000 targets, decapitating much of Iran’s senior leadership, and shattering the country’s air defenses, air force, navy, and missile forces. As Tehran fired back, U.S. crews intercepted hundreds of missiles and drones aimed at ships and Gulf bases.

This is the difference between disruption and command. Some middle powers can make stronger armies bleed. But none can consistently track thousands of targets, move forces across long distances, protect them in transit, refuel and rearm them, fuse intelligence across domains, and keep fighting for weeks or months far from home. Even successful resistance usually depends on a larger system. Ukraine has fought brilliantly, for example, but only by plugging into a Western network of money, intelligence, air defense, training, communications, and ammunition.

THE MIDDLE CANNOT HOLD

If middle powers are back on the menu, the obvious response is to band together. That was Carney’s message at Davos, and the impulse is understandable. Coalitions can amplify middle powers’ voices and give them leverage on specific issues. But they cannot turn them into great powers, or guarantee them a permanent seat at the table.

The first problem is scale. The world is not multipolar. On the core measures of power, the United States towers over the field, China usually ranks second, and everyone else is crowded far below. The gap between the top two powers and the rest is much larger than the gaps between the middle powers themselves.

That steep drop-off means that even the broadest conceivable middle-power coalitions cannot form a pole. Take the Harvard Belfer Center’s designation of 13 “middle powers”: Brazil, Egypt, India, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Nigeria, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, South Africa, Turkey, the UAE, and Vietnam. Together, these countries’ economies still make up less than half of U.S. GDP, about two-fifths of the U.S. consumer market, roughly one-quarter of U.S. military spending, and almost none of the world’s leading high-tech revenue. Belfer itself calls them “not a coherent bloc.”

Even fantasy coalitions fall short. Take the countries ranked third through tenth on any major measure of power—GDP at market exchange rates, consumer market size, military spending, or high-tech revenue—and combine them. They still do not match the United States. Such blocs would be wildly implausible, requiring some of Washington’s closest allies to align with Russia. Yet even on paper, they would have a smaller GDP than the United States, a consumer market one-quarter smaller, military spending only two-thirds as large, and high-tech revenue less than half as large.

But size is only the first problem. The more embedded obstacle is politics. Middle-power coalitions face a basic tradeoff: the larger they become, the more weight they carry, but the harder they are to hold together. Small clubs can move quickly but lack enough mass to matter. Large ones gain heft only by also adding veto points, rivalries, and free riders. To overcome these problems, successful coalitions usually need an anchor: a state inside the coalition that is willing and able to lead it toward a shared goal by absorbing costs, reassuring fence sitters, and punishing defectors. The United Kingdom played that role against Napoleon. The British and, later, the Soviets, did so against Hitler until the United States entered World War II. No middle power performs that role today. As a result, no serious middle-power coalition exists.

The easiest way to build a middle-power coalition is under superpower protection. But that solution creates a paradox. Hegemonic shelter helps middle powers pool resources and overcome internal divisions, yet it also saps their ability to stand alone. Like a greenhouse, it lets a fragile garden grow while leaving it unfit for harsher weather.

The EU embodies this paradox. Rich and deeply institutionalized, it looks like the world’s most promising middle-power coalition. But the EU is a product of U.S. hegemony, not an alternative to it. U.S. protection made European integration possible by suppressing the continent’s old security dilemmas. In doing so, however, it also smothered Europe’s will and capacity for hard power. Instead, in the post–Cold War era, Europe became a welfare superpower, spending less than two percent of GDP on defense but roughly 25 percent on social protection—more than half the world’s total, despite having just five percent of its population. The result is extreme dependence on the United States for intelligence, targeting, refueling, air defense, logistics, munitions, and long-range strike capabilities. Europe has repeatedly struggled to manage crises on its own continent, from the Balkans to Ukraine, let alone project power overseas.

Europe also grew accustomed to buying what it needed from a U.S.-protected global economy rather than building industrial strength at home. It focused on rulemaking, assuming others would adopt its standards, but instead regulated itself into energy vulnerability and technological stagnation. It shuttered nuclear plants, banned fracking, and now imports 60 percent of its energy. Before the war in Ukraine, Russia supplied roughly half of Europe’s gas and coal imports and more than a quarter of its oil. Since then, Europe has traded dependence on Russia for deeper reliance on the United States.

In technology, only four of the world’s 50 largest technology companies by market capitalization are European, whereas roughly 30 are American. Between 2013 and 2023, Europe’s share of global tech revenue fell from 22 percent to 18 percent, while the U.S. share rose from 30 percent to 38 percent. Europe polices digital capitalism but rarely produces it. With no firms that reach the scale of Amazon, Google, Meta, or Microsoft, much of Europe’s digital economy now runs on American platforms: cloud computing, business software, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence systems, smartphone operating systems, and payment systems.

The overall trend is relative decline. In 2008, the EU economy was larger than the United States’ and produced 25 percent of global GDP. By 2024, it was one-third smaller than the United States’, and its global share of GDP had fallen to 17 percent.

Consensus rules often reduce ASEAN to a diplomatic waiting room.

Other middle-power coalitions are weaker still. BRICS, which began as BRIC before adding South Africa, has since expanded to include even more new members and partner countries, including Iran. But rather than becoming a counterweight to great-power coercion, BRICS became a grievance caucus, bringing middle powers together with China and Russia—the very bullies many members want to hedge against. Its members resent Western dominance but also distrust one another: India fears China, Iran clashes with Gulf states, and most prefer flexibility to bloc discipline. When the United States and Israel attacked Iran this year, BRICS could not even agree on a joint statement.

The Association of Southeast Asian Nations, now an 11-country organization, is no stronger. Its members share no common threat, strategy, or economic base. Vietnam and the Philippines fear China while Cambodia and Laos depend on it, Indonesia guards its autonomy, Singapore hedges, and Myanmar is in civil war. Consensus rules let any member block action, often reducing ASEAN to a diplomatic waiting room.

Smaller groupings look more promising only because they do less. OPEC once showed that middle powers can wield real leverage when they control a concentrated, indispensable resource. But OPEC is a single-commodity cartel, not a geopolitical bloc. Its members want high oil prices, not a common political order—and even that narrow bargain is fraying, as Angola, Qatar, and the UAE have exited the organization. The Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership is a trade pact, not a strategic coalition. Caucuses within bigger international organizations, such as the Ottawa Group in the World Trade Organization, are useful technical forums but not substitutes for power. The most effective minilaterals, by contrast, simply prove the rule. The Quad links Australia, India, Japan, and the United States in security cooperation. Pax Silica is a technology supply chain initiative. Both work because Washington anchors them.

That leaves the option Carney put forward in his Davos speech: “variable geometry,” a technical label for improvising coalitions issue by issue. But that is not a middle-power order. It is world politics as usual: states scrambling under pressure while stronger powers divide, bribe, threaten, and punish any coalition that tries to route around them. Some scholars imagine an à la carte order, in which middle powers shop freely for security here, technology there, and market access elsewhere. But the world is not a mall. It is a state of nature. After Europe built a mechanism called INSTEX in 2019 to bypass U.S. sanctions and continue trade with Iran, Washington threatened its users with exile from the dollar system. That same year, after Turkey bought Russian air defenses, Washington expelled it from the F-35 program. In 2025, as India continued buying Russian oil despite U.S. warnings not to, Washington hammered it with 50 percent tariffs.

China enforces its hierarchy just as aggressively. It pressed Cambodia to block ASEAN from criticizing Beijing’s military expansion in the South China Sea, punished Lithuania’s outreach to Taiwan by restricting trade and targeting Lithuanian inputs in global supply chains, and hit Australia with tariffs and informal bans on barley, wine, beef, coal, cotton, and lobsters after Canberra sought an inquiry into the origins of COVID. It also forced Vietnam to halt offshore gas projects with Spanish-, Emirati-, Russian-, and Japanese-linked firms by threatening confrontation and swarming drilling sites with maritime militia vessels. In a world riven by great-power conflict, variable geometry does not protect middle powers. It exposes them, because any coalition strong enough to matter is visible enough to punish.

PICK YOUR PATRON

If middle powers cannot stand alone, form a pole, or hide among ad hoc coalitions, they must choose a larger system to lean into. This does not require blind obedience. They can still trade widely and talk to everyone. But on the core issues of power—whose weapons to buy, whose intelligence to share, whose banks to rely on, whose chips and cloud platforms to build on, whose energy networks to join, and whose sanctions to enforce—they increasingly will have to pick a side. Hedging works when threats are distant and great powers tolerate ambiguity. It falters when rivalry hardens and both sides start asking the same question: are you with us or against us? For middle powers, the challenge is no longer how to avoid choosing. It is how to choose a patron without becoming a pawn.

Alignment is not submission. It is a strategy for turning niches into leverage. Alone, middle powers’ assets—ports, bases, chipmaking tools, mineral deposits, drone industries, shipyards—may not be enough to keep a country safe. But within a larger alliance, those niches can become bargaining chips for what middle powers lack: protection, intelligence, technology, capital, market access, and influence over strategy. The point is not to escape dependence, which is usually impossible, but to make dependence reciprocal. A country that proves itself useful to a superpower can become a partner the superpower consults, arms, funds, and defends.

The easiest way to build a middle-power coalition is under superpower protection.

Japan shows how this works. As Michael Green recently explained in Foreign Affairs, Tokyo is not trying to replace American power in Asia; it is making itself indispensable to it. Japan gives Washington what it needs to compete in the region: local bases, technology, industrial capacity, ship repair, missile production, help organizing coalitions, and regional legitimacy, by making U.S. strategy look less like outside intervention and more like a coalition led in part by a major Asian democracy. In return, Japan gets access to the only power large enough to balance China, plus a stronger voice in how that power is used.

Finland and Sweden made a similar choice in Europe. They joined NATO not because they had forgotten how to defend themselves but because national resilience works better when backed by American power. NATO gained highly capable northern militaries, and Finland and Sweden got Article 5 protection. Australia, Poland, the Philippines, and South Korea are variations on the same theme: each is building national strength while making itself more valuable within a U.S.-led network.

This is why alliances, unlike most international security organizations, actually work. Collective security bodies, such as the defunct League of Nations and the United Nations, are designed to ask states to defend rules in the abstract—against any aggressor, in any location, and whether or not they see a direct stake. Variable geometry is even more tenuous, asking states essentially to wing it as dangers emerge. Alliances, on the other hand, are far more practical. They name the threat, pool capabilities, assign roles, and build habits of cooperation before crises hit. Their glue is not universal goodwill. It is common danger. That may be an ugly motivator, but states cooperate more reliably when they know what they are cooperating against.

Choosing the right alignment, however, is not automatic. Middle powers should not blindly attach themselves to the strongest or most familiar state. They should ask a harder question: which great power threatens their security, prosperity, and autonomy more? The answers will vary. Some states face invasion. Others face economic coercion, technological dependence, political interference, or abandonment by an unreliable patron. A few large middle powers, such as India, may preserve more room to maneuver than most. But as great-power rivalry intensifies, even the strongest middle powers will have to decide which danger they most need to escape and which larger system gives them the most refuge.

Any coalition strong enough to matter is visible enough to punish.

For most middle powers, this is a choice among lesser evils, but it should be an easy one. The United States is an increasingly difficult patron. It bullies allies with tariffs, sanctions, export controls, demands for military access to their territories, and sudden policy swings. But it still offers what no other power can match: protection by the only military that can fight major faraway wars; access to the world’s deepest capital markets, largest consumer base, and leading innovation hubs; and entry into a large club of wealthy, capable allies. Just as important, American power runs through democratic institutions that outsiders can sometimes influence. Allies can lobby Congress, mobilize firms, shape media debates, and logroll with other U.S. partners. They may not win, but they can play.

China offers a thinner bargain. Beijing can build roads, finance ports, buy commodities, and supply cheap goods and industrial parts. For some states, that is useful. But beyond loans, infrastructure, and leverage against the West, Beijing offers little: no security umbrella, reserve currency, or open political channels through which weaker partners can bargain. Its power works by enclosure. It finances the port, supplies the construction companies, builds the network, processes the mineral, floods the market with exports, and seeks to buy less over time. What begins as development can become vassalage.

Ultimately, middle powers cannot choose whether or not to live in a hierarchical world. They must choose which hierarchy gives them the most room to maneuver. The danger is mistaking the performance of autonomy for the substance of power—celebrating summits, forums, and rousing speeches while the real levers of money, technology, energy, and force accumulate in stronger hands. Security will not come from standing alone or stitching together ad hoc coalitions but from bargaining effectively inside a larger system. Middle powers are not free agents in a flat world. But they can still prosper by partnering with a great power in an increasingly unequal one.

articles.title: Japan’s Point of No Return
articles.content:
过去十年间，日本已逐渐摆脱其和平主义身份。在二战后承诺仅维持一支小型军队之后，东京如今正在建设真正具备能力的武装力量和规模可观的国防工业基础。例如，2018年12月，日本宣布计划改造出云级驱逐舰，使其能够起降F-35B战斗机——这实际上将赋予日本自1945年以来首艘航空母舰——并采购147架F-35战斗机。2023年，日本允许其企业开始销售某些进攻性武器和武器零部件。上个月，东京取消了大部分剩余的武器出口限制，包括驱逐舰、导弹和战斗机。这场日本转向理应在华盛顿受到广泛欢迎，因为美国长期以来一直寻求让这个富裕的东亚盟友增加国防开支。这些举措旨在加强同盟关系，日本官员对美国同行的伙伴关系依然高度认同。例如，一个更强大的日本对于阻止中国进攻台湾至关重要。面对一支不仅有美国和台湾力量、还有装备精良的日本军队的联盟，北京更不可能与之对抗。越来越多的迹象表明，中国必须假设东京可能会介入该岛的冲突，这意味着任何台海战争都将涉及日本基地、导弹、传感器、防空系统和后勤网络，使快速胜利难以实现。

然而，东京的努力是否最终会加强美日伙伴关系仍是一个悬而未决的问题——答案高度取决于华盛顿如何回应。自2025年唐纳德·特朗普重返白宫以来，美国一直以交易性条款来定义美国同盟关系，日本也不例外。例如，2025年4月，特朗普对日本商品征收25%的关税。（双方最终达成协议，将大多数税率设定为15%，随后最高法院裁定这些关税不符合规定。）特朗普此前还要求日本为继续驻扎美军支付大约四倍的费用。他称日本“被宠坏了”，“占了我们三四十年的便宜”。如果美国继续抨击日本，东京确实可能走上一条更加独立的发展道路。毕竟，日本不断增加的国防开支和日益增长的实力是一种保险政策，有助于在美国动摇时保护日本的安全。

华盛顿应该放弃从日本国库中尽可能多榨取的努力。相反，它必须寻找利用东京实力的方式——例如通过在军事生产方面进行合作，或更好地连接两国在太平洋的指挥链。这可能会让一些习惯了作为更强大伙伴的美国官员感到担忧。但美国主导地位的些许丧失完全值得换取这些收益。多年来，美国一直在寻找更好地约束北京的方法。一个能够在危机初期坚守阵地并帮助美国反击的日本是一个绝佳的解决方案。

日本长期以来一直在考虑摆脱其和平主义姿态，但其安全转型直到2015年才真正开始。那一年，日本首相安倍晋三通过了立法，允许日本自卫队在有限情况下使用武力，即所谓的“集体自卫权”，例如当对美国的攻击威胁到日本生存时。（此前，日本仅在被攻击时才允许使用武力。）该改革在通过时极具争议，引发了愤怒和抗议。但它最终生效了，此后日本进一步放宽了对军队的约束。例如，2017年，安倍表示东京将不再把非正式的GDP百分之一国防开支上限视为不可逾越的红线。2018年，日本创建了水陆机动团，这是一支海军陆战队风格的部队，旨在保卫和夺回偏远岛屿。日本2022年的国家安全战略和防卫力量整备计划承诺获取反击能力，包括射程更远的导弹。

似乎没有人比日本现任首相高市早苗更致力于这一转变。作为安倍的得意门生，高市于去年10月上任后，立即着手扩大日本的军事态势。11月7日，她在日本议会表示，中国对台湾的攻击可能威胁到日本的生存，这比任何前任都更明确地发出信号：若该岛遭受攻击，东京可能根据2015年安全法使用军事力量。作为回应，愤怒的北京指责高市试图让日本回归殖民时代，并警告称如果日本不收回这一声明，将面临严重后果。高市拒绝让步。中国随后部分兑现了威胁，劝阻中国公民赴日旅行，并暂停进口日本海鲜。中国限制向日本军事实体出口军民两用物品，甚至威胁对向日本提供中国来源的两用物品的中国境外组织或个人采取法律行动，后来还将数十家日本上市公司和组织列入出口管制清单和观察名单。12月，据日本官员称，中国战斗机甚至用火控雷达锁定了日本军用飞机。

高市立场坚定，她拒绝让步似乎提升了她的支持率。这一争端的结局说明了胁迫中国的悖论：北京越是试图压制，就越面临反弹。（其他政府，包括澳大利亚、印度和台湾，也因批评北京而面临越来越大的压力，同样选择坚持立场。）

东京目前计划到2027年将国防开支增加到GDP的约2%，并通过在西南诸岛增加导弹部队、防空系统、弹药库、燃料储备、加固掩体以及允许飞机和地面部队在琉球群岛分散部署的设施来加强安全。东京正在投资弹药储备，扩大无人系统，并放宽出口和国防工业合作的限制，以便日本企业能够与合作伙伴（如美国制造商）共同生产，而不仅仅是向他们采购。

日本不再是在边缘松动约束或进行一次性采购；它正在转变整个防御态势。高市的后继者可能会放缓某些项目或重新调整一些优先事项，但扭转日本整备的整体轨迹几乎是不可能的。这需要取消多年期采购合同、缩减生产线、废除新的出口规则，并削弱正在全国西南地区建设的部队和基地。此外，鉴于这些改革的受欢迎程度，日本不太可能选出想要废除它们的领导人。东京大多数政界人士的辩论不再是日本是否应该加强自身安全；而是应该以多快的速度推进。

日本的做法是对被中国胁迫的国家的自然回应。但这也是对可能出现的美国退缩的担忧的回应。这个国家并不是试图取代美国成为该地区的安全提供者；它的目标是帮助预防或遏制华盛顿可能犹豫不决——或认为支持盟友代价过高——的区域危机。对东京来说，这种保护尤为重要，因为它靠近东亚最危险的爆发点和关键供应链瓶颈。

东京的努力也尤其重要，因为台海危机可能始于中国试图分裂美国和日本决策者的行动。作为封锁或全面进攻的序幕，北京可能切断维持威慑所需的工业投入的获取：稀土、特种材料、传感器、无人机零部件、芯片相关工具以及民用和国防生产商使用的两用技术。这种压力可能推动日本和美国走向不同方向。东京可能感到被迫迅速行动，保护其领土、保持海上通道畅通、防止台湾或东中国海既成事实。相比之下，如果干预威胁到美国经济或扰乱美国工业依赖的供应链，美国官员可能会犹豫不决。

日本目前的努力正是对这一问题的回应。通过明确承诺支持台湾并建设其军事力量，东京发出的信号是：即使美国犹豫，它也不会回避保卫该岛。通过深化与美国日常作战协调——通过更复杂的联合演习、扩大的情报共享以及自卫队和美国驻日军队之间更紧密的规划——东京希望确保华盛顿最终也会参与。

如果美国决定不采取行动，日本能够为邻国提供的保护是有限的。其近期变革的目标不是让日本成为地区超级大国，而仅仅是争取时间。这些步骤有助于确保中国不能以既成事实的方式吞并台湾或夺取日本管辖的领土。在台海或东中国海危机中，初期军事态势将至关重要。如果日本能够保持琉球群岛和主岛的机场正常运转、分散部队、维持弹药、保护港口并在美国援助到来之前保持通讯，中国将更难取得它可能需要的快速决定性胜利。事实上，如果北京只是相信东京能够完成所有这些事情——并在保卫自己领土的同时支持美国的最终行动——中国可能根本不会尝试对邻国采取行动。

东京知道，中国试图胁迫的不仅仅是自己。因此，日本一些最具影响力的投资投向弹药、燃料、维护和修理用品、掩体、后勤船只、网络弹性以及其他类型的保护性硬件和软件。尽管这些投资可能不会成为轰动性新闻，但它们决定着一支军队在危机爆发时能否正常运转。

批评日本安全转向的人士担心，这些步骤虽然大部分是防御性的，但会增加升级风险。如果日本的新能力与美国协调不当，由模糊的交战规则或不精确的角色分工管理，情况确实可能如此。但只要日本将自身力量建设作为美国力量的补充而非替代，这种整备就不太可能刺激北京发动攻击。事实上，无所作为更可能引发冲突。毕竟，如果日本显得软弱和犹豫不决，北京将更容易受到试探。

东京还试图通过建立更强的外交和行动网络来加强威慑。与华盛顿的同盟将始终是其安全战略的核心，但日本政府现在正与澳大利亚、印度、欧洲国家、东南亚国家甚至韩国——后者与前殖民宗主国通常关系紧张——更密切地合作。这些关系使中国更难孤立日本，通过创建更多渠道让东京与合作伙伴进行规划，开展联合训练以提高自卫队的战备状态，并使供应链多样化以支持贸易。反过来，其合作伙伴自身也获得了某种程度的保护。对他们来说，一个更有能力的日本不是威胁而是稳定器——是资源和技术的来源，是军事训练和海岸警卫队支持以及有助于支撑国防制造的工业能力的来源。

因此，日本的安全整备不仅仅是一个关于东京和华盛顿或东京和北京的双边故事。它是全球安全再平衡的一部分。日本新的经济关系对其合作伙伴尤为重要。东京知道，中国不仅仅用经济限制胁迫自己，它深知北京通过将经济相互依赖武器化还能造成多大的破坏。例如，中国在稀土和各种其他重要军事材料的开采和特别是精炼方面占据主导地位。中国1月和2月的出口管制——限制军民两用商品的出口——凸显了经济依赖关系多么迅速地成为胁迫工具。尽管如此，东京在缓解大部分此类危险并帮助其他国家做到这一点方面处于有利地位。



它在维持全球半导体、电池、先进制造、机器人和其他国防材料供应的高科技生产网络中扮演核心角色。日本在东南亚、欧洲以及美国都享有信誉，可以帮助这些国家在其供应链面临压力时重新设计和调整供应链。因此，东京可以帮助防止这些国家各自仓促加强供应链时可能产生的昂贵冗余。东京的战略决策旨在惠及华盛顿。然而，美国有可能浪费日本的协助。这是因为华盛顿突然似乎对合作伙伴的福祉不感兴趣。相反，它似乎更关心从他们那里获取经济让步。例如，特朗普坚持认为，如果美国的北约盟友不为自身安全支付更多费用，美国就"不会保卫他们"，而他推行全面关税的尝试忽视了长期互利的美中贸易关系。这种做法是自我挫败的。将安全承诺与双边关税、技术例外条款或不相关的商业让步挂钩，会给盟友寻找不经过美国的市场、融资渠道和供应链的理由。迄今为止，尽管美国行为异常，日本在很大程度上仍站在华盛顿一边。然而，如果美国官员不改变做法，东京可以利用其更大的能力进行更艰难的讨价还价，并在华盛顿直接影响之外更多地塑造其安全政策。通过将更多国防采购和联合开发转向澳大利亚和欧洲，日本将创建绕过美国及其制造商的库存和供应链。东京可以开始抵制更高的驻军费用分摊，并通过不涉及华盛顿的多边论坛协调更多区域规划。这些行为不会使日本反美，但会让美国对东京的影响力减弱。

因此，华盛顿必须停止对日本施压。相反，它需要与其盟友更密切地合作。它可以从将日本的军事投资视为改善负担分担的举措开始——这当然正是它们的本质。然后，它可以与日本官员合作，建立一个有助于加强两国的联盟供应链。例如，两国可以利用其联合制造能力，共同生产导弹、防空拦截器、无人驾驶载具、传感器、船舶维修能力和天基海上感知技术等关键系统。它们还可以创建弹药和关键材料的共享储备。这将使中国更难威慑这两个国家中的任何一个或在它们之间制造分裂。

美国政策制定者还应加强与日本同行的沟通渠道。两国政府尤其应该投资于更清晰的联合决策路径，以便在危机发生时能够做出快速有效的选择。美国军方应该与日本自卫队一起演练紧急情况，将升级后的驻日美军司令部与日本联合作战司令部对接，并构建联合升级管理机制。作为其中的一部分，华盛顿应该鼓励东京与北京保持沟通渠道畅通，特别是在关系紧张时。

随着中国变得更加咄咄逼人和胁迫，日本将继续扩大其防御体系。对于美国来说，这是一个需要抓住的机会——事实上，这也是许多美国官员长期以来的要求。如果当前美国政府继续将日本视为客户，华盛顿可能会发现，其最亲密的亚洲盟友已经学会在美国帮助下——利用美国鼓励日本培养的那些合作伙伴——绕过美国行事。但如果华盛顿将一个更强大的日本视为真正的合作伙伴，并将其牢牢留在联盟体系内，美国在亚洲的影响力将比以往任何时候都更大。
articles.summary:
Over the last ten years, Japan has shed its pacifist identity. After promising, post–World War II, to maintain only a tiny military, Tokyo is now building up truly capable armed forces and a sizable defense industrial base. In December 2018, for example, the country announced plans to modify its Izumo-class destroyers so they could operate F-35B fighters—effectively giving Japan its first aircraft carrier since 1945—and to buy 147 F-35 fighter jets. In 2023, it allowed its companies to start selling certain offensive weapons and weapons parts. And last month, Tokyo scrapped most of the remaining limits on arms exports, including destroyers, missiles, and jets.

Japan’s pivot should be widely welcomed in Washington, which has long sought to get its wealthy East Asian ally to spend more on defense. These moves are designed to strengthen the alliance, as Japanese officials remain deeply committed to their partnership with their U.S. peers. A stronger Japan, for example, could be critical in deterring China from attacking Taiwan. Beijing is less likely to confront a coalition that features not just U.S. and Taiwanese forces but well-equipped Japanese ones, as well. And increasingly, China has to assume that Tokyo would enter a conflict over the island, meaning that any Taiwan war would involve Japanese bases, missiles, sensors, air defenses, and logistics networks, making a quick victory hard to achieve.

Still, whether Tokyo’s efforts actually end up strengthening the U.S.-Japanese partnership is an open question—and the answer is highly contingent on how Washington reacts. Ever since U.S. President Donald Trump returned to office in 2025, the United States has been casting U.S. alliances in transactional terms, the Japanese one among them. In April 2025, for instance, Trump slapped 25 percent tariffs on Japanese goods. (The two sides eventually struck a deal setting most rates to 15 percent, before the Supreme Court ruled the tariffs impermissible.) Trump also previously demanded that Japan pay roughly four times as much to the United States in exchange for continuing to host U.S. troops. He called Japan “spoiled” for “having ripped us off for 30, 40 years.” If the United States continues to lambast Japan, then Tokyo indeed might forge a more independent path. Its increased defense spending and growing strength, after all, act as an insurance policy that helps keep Japan safe in the event the United States wavers.

Washington, then, should abandon its efforts to extract as much as it can from Japanese coffers. It must instead look for ways to capitalize on Tokyo’s strength—such as by working together on military production, or better connecting their command chains in the Pacific. This might make some U.S. officials, accustomed to being by far the more powerful partner, wary. But any loss of American dominance is well worth the benefits. For years, the United States has been searching for a way to better constrain Beijing. A Japan that can hold the line early in a crisis and help the United States fight back is an excellent solution.

BECOMING STRONG

Japan has long toyed with the idea of shedding its pacifist posture, but its security evolution only began in earnest in 2015. That year, Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe passed legislation that permitted Japan’s Self-Defense Forces to use force in limited cases of what it called “collective self-defense,” such as if an attack on the United States threatened Japan’s survival. (Previously, Japan allowed the use of force only when Japan itself was attacked.) The reform was hugely controversial at the time of passage, prompting outrage and protests. But it went into effect, and Japan has further loosened the constraints on its military in the time since. In 2017, for example, Abe signaled that Tokyo would no longer treat its informal one-percent-of-GDP ceiling on defense spending as inviolable. In 2018, Japan created the Amphibious Rapid Deployment Brigade, a marine-style unit designed to defend and retake remote islands. And Japan's 2022 National Security Strategy and Defense Buildup Program committed the country to acquiring counterstrike capabilities, including longer-range missiles.

No one seems more committed to this shift than Japan’s current prime minister, Sanae Takaichi. An Abe protégé, Takaichi took office last October and wasted little time expanding Japan’s military posture. On November 7, she told the Japanese parliament that a Chinese attack on Taiwan could threaten Japan’s survival, signaling more clearly than any of her predecessors that Tokyo might use military force under the 2015 security laws if an assault on the island occurred. In response, a furious Beijing accused Takaichi of trying to return Japan to its colonial past and warned that the country would face serious consequences if it failed to retract the statement. Takaichi refused. China then acted on parts of its threat, discouraging Chinese citizens from traveling to Japan and suspending imports of seafood from Japan. It restricted exports of dual-use items to Japanese military entities, going as far as to threaten legal action against organizations or individuals outside of China that provided Chinese-origin dual-use items to Japan, and later placed dozens of Japanese listed companies and organizations on export-control lists and watch lists. In December, according to Japanese officials, Chinese fighter jets even locked fire-control radars on Japanese military aircraft. Takaichi has stood firm, and her refusal to back down appears to have boosted her popularity.

The outcome of this spat illustrates the paradox of Chinese coercion: the harder Beijing tries to crack down, the more it faces blowback. (Other governments, including Australia, India, and Taiwan, have also come under mounting Chinese pressure for criticizing Beijing and have also responded by holding firm.) Tokyo is now set to spend roughly two percent of its GDP on defense by 2027, and it is strengthening security on its southwestern islands by adding missile units, air defenses, ammunition depots, fuel stores, hardened shelters, and facilities that allow aircraft and ground units to disperse across the Ryukyu Islands. It is investing in munitions stocks, expanding unmanned systems, and loosening constraints on exports and defense industrial cooperation so that Japanese companies can build with partners, such as manufacturers in the United States, rather than merely buying from them.

Japan is no longer loosening restraints around the edges or making one-off purchases; it is transforming its entire defense posture. Takaichi’s successors could slow some programs or reorder some priorities, but reversing the overall trajectory of Japan’s buildup is nearly impossible. It would require canceling multiyear procurement contracts, shrinking production lines, undoing new export rules, and weakening units and bases that are being built across the country’s southwest. Moreover, given the popularity of these reforms, the country is unlikely to elect leaders who want to undo them. The debate in Tokyo among most politicians is no longer about whether Japan should bolster its own security; it is about how quickly it should do so.

DETER AND DELAY

Japan’s efforts are the natural response of a state squeezed by Chinese coercion. But they are also the response to fears of a possible U.S. pullback. The country is not trying to replace the United States as the region's security provider; it is aiming to help prevent or contain a regional crisis in which Washington might hesitate—or decide that the costs of supporting its allies are too high. For Tokyo, such protection is particularly essential given its proximity to East Asia’s most dangerous flashpoints and critical supply chain chokepoints.

Tokyo’s efforts are also particularly essential because a Taiwan crisis might begin with a Chinese effort to split U.S. and Japanese decision-makers. As a prelude to a blockade or outright assault, Beijing might choke off access to the industrial inputs required to sustain deterrence: rare earths, specialty materials, sensors, drone components, semiconductor-related tools, and dual-use technologies used by both civilian industry and defense producers. That pressure could push Japan and the United States in different directions. Tokyo might feel compelled to act quickly to protect its territory, keep sea lanes open, and prevent a fait accompli around Taiwan or the East China Sea. U.S. officials, by contrast, might hesitate if intervention threatened the American economy or disrupted supply chains on which U.S. industry depends. Japan’s current efforts are an answer to that problem. By expressly committing itself to Taiwan and building up its military, Tokyo is signaling that it will not shy away from defending the island even if the United States hesitates. And by deepening day-to-day operational coordination with the United States—through more complex joint exercises, expanded intelligence sharing, and closer planning between the Self-Defense Forces and U.S. Forces Japan—Tokyo hopes to make sure Washington will ultimately get involved, too.

There are limits to how much protection Japan can offer its neighbors if the United States decides to do nothing. The goal of its recent changes is not to make Japan a regional superpower but simply to buy time. These steps could help ensure that China cannot roll over Taiwan or seize Japanese-administered territory as a fait accompli. In a crisis over Taiwan or the East China Sea, initial military posturing will matter tremendously. If Japan can keep airfields in the Ryukyus and main islands operational, disperse forces, sustain munitions, protect ports, and maintain communications before U.S. help arrives, China will have much more trouble scoring the kind of quick, decisive victory it may need. In fact, if Beijing simply believes that Tokyo can do all these things—and support eventual U.S. operations while defending its own territory—China may not make any attempt at its neighbor.

Tokyo knows that it is not the only country China has tried to coerce.

As a result, some of Japan’s most consequential investments have been in munitions, fuel, maintenance and repair supplies, shelters, logistics vessels, cyber-resilience, and other kinds of protective hardware and software. Although these kinds of investments may not make dramatic headlines, they determine whether a military can function when a crisis breaks out. Critics of Japan’s security pivot worry that these steps, although mostly defensive, will increase the risk of escalation. If Japan’s new capabilities are poorly coordinated with the United States, governed by unclear rules of engagement or imprecise role-sharing, they might. But so long as Japan builds up its forces as a complement to American power, and not a replacement for it, this buildup is unlikely to spur an attack from Beijing. In fact, doing nothing is more likely to prompt a conflict. If Japan appears weak and indecisive, after all, Beijing will be more tempted to test it.

Tokyo has also sought to strengthen deterrence by building stronger diplomatic and operational networks. Its alliance with Washington will always be at the center of its security strategy, but Japan’s government is now working more closely with Australia, India, European states, Southeast Asian states, and even South Korea—which has typically had tense relations with its former colonial master. These relationships make it harder for China to isolate Japan by creating more channels for Tokyo to plan with partners, conduct joint training to improve the readiness of Japan’s Self-Defense Forces, and diversify supply chains to bolster trade. Its partners, in turn, gain a measure of protection themselves. To them, a more capable Japan is not a threat but a ballast—a source of resources and technology, of military training and coast guard support, and of the industrial capacity that helps underpin defense manufacturing. Japan's security buildup is therefore bigger than a bilateral story about Tokyo and Washington or Tokyo and Beijing. It is part of a global security rebalancing.

Japan’s new economic relationships are particularly essential for its partners. Tokyo knows that it is not the only country China has tried to coerce with economic restrictions, and it is aware of just how much more havoc Beijing can wreak by weaponizing economic interdependence. China, for example, dominates the mining and, particularly, refining of rare earths and various other important military materials. Its January and February export controls, which restricted exports of dual military-civilian-use goods, underscored how quickly economic dependencies can become tools of coercion.

Nevertheless, Tokyo is well placed to mitigate much of this danger and help other countries do so, as well. It plays a central role within the high-tech production networks that sustain global supplies of semiconductors, batteries, advanced manufacturing, robotics, and other defense materials. With credibility in Southeast Asia and Europe as well as in the United States, Japan can help these countries redesign and retool their supply chains when they come under duress. Tokyo can therefore help prevent the kind of expensive redundancies that might occur if these states rush to fortify their supply chains separately, rather than working together.

FRIEND, NOT CLIENT

Tokyo’s strategic decisions are designed to benefit Washington. Yet the United States risks squandering Japan’s assistance. That is because Washington suddenly appears uninterested in its partners’ well-being. Instead, it seems more concerned with extracting financial concessions from them. Trump, for instance, insisted that if the United States’ NATO allies did not pay more for their own security, then the United States was “not going to defend them,” and his attempts to institute universal tariffs ignored long-standing, mutually beneficial U.S. trade partnerships. This approach is self-defeating. Linking security commitments to bilateral tariffs, technology carve-outs, or unrelated commercial concessions gives allies a reason to look for markets, financing channels, and supply routes that do not run through the United States.

Thus far, Japan has largely stood by Washington despite this extraordinary behavior. Nonetheless, if U.S. officials do not change course, Tokyo could use its greater capacity to bargain harder and shape more of its security policy beyond Washington’s direct influence. By shifting more defense procurement and codevelopment toward Australia and Europe, Japan would create stockpiles and supply chains that circumvent the United States and its manufacturers. Tokyo could start resisting higher host-nation support payments and coordinate more regional planning through multilateral forums that do not involve Washington. These acts would not make Japan anti-American, but they would leave the United States with less influence over Tokyo.

Washington, then, must stop pressuring Japan. Instead, it needs to work more closely with its ally. It can start by treating the country’s military investments as moves toward improved burden sharing—which is, of course, what they are. It could then work with Japanese officials to build an allied supply chain that helps strengthen both states. The two countries could use their joint manufacturing capacity, for example, to coproduce key systems such as missiles, air-defense interceptors, uncrewed vehicles, sensors, ship-repair capacity, and space-based maritime awareness technologies. They could also create shared stockpiles for munitions and critical materials. This would make it harder for China to intimidate either country or drive a wedge between them.

U.S. policymakers ought to also improve communications channels with their Japanese counterparts. The two governments should, in particular, invest in clearer pathways for joint decision-making so that they can make quick and effective choices in the event of a crisis. It behooves the U.S. military to rehearse emergency scenarios with Japan’s Self-Defense Forces, connect the upgraded U.S. Forces Japan headquarters to Japan’s Joint Operations Command, and construct joint mechanisms for managing escalation. As part of doing so, Washington should encourage Tokyo to keep its channels with Beijing open, especially when relations get tense.

As China becomes more aggressive and coercive, Japan will keep expanding its defense apparatus.
